speaker: interviewer
text: It is March 3, 1996. So you were describing your arrival at Auschwitz and how you tried to shut out the outside world to an extent to keep your, your sanity. You mentioned a week after you arrived at Auschwitz that your mother followed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out that she had arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found because-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] let's come back for a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: During the time when we were there trying to stay alive they did the most heinous thing. They were hanging people. We don't know what they did, but we had to watch it. This is something very bad to watch when somebody is getting hanged. And this is how you learned to see it because people watched you. You had to watch it. You see it, and you didn't see it. And during that kind of happening I realized what they do-- what they try to do is to undermine your sanity in any way they could so that you either end up dead or a Muselman, as they call it, or incapable of doing anything, and they don't need to food you anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now Muselman meaning what, for people who are not familiar with the term.

speaker: interviewee
text: Muselman means somebody who [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] walks like this, looks on the floor and is ready to die. The mind is already about to die. And we decide-- you know, all the ones we knew, we decided to, to pep talk us. We saw it, but it's gone. It's past. Luckily we didn't know who it was. And this is how we sustained ourselves through that time. And these were very, very difficult days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then they made a mistake, and they put us in the gas chambers. And we tried to protest, but, you know, you can't protest anything until-- we said we wanted to poll this person and that person, and Mengele, and ba ba ba, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally they said, it is a mistake, and they opened the doors again. And from that gas, which had already emitted, you were completely drowsy, and if you had asked me my name then I would have not known. And all they did is they put us on the floor, ice cold floor. It was in November, outside. And lying there, suddenly I heard a certain cough, and it was a cough of my stepfather. And I kind of moved around, and I saw him in the man's camp there, not walking alone but aided by somebody else I know. And these two men were enemies. And they were going to the gas chamber in the man's quarters. I was terrible, and then I realized if he is there, my mother must be there. It was just a question where, but she was never in my surrounding. So I only knew about it that she was there positively when I came back to Hamburg and I asked her exactly what date did you get to Auschwitz. And she told me. She asked me why and I said, oh, no reason. I didn't want her to know that I knew that I saw when my stepfather was pushed into the gas chambers-- that I didn't want to know, her to know at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back just a little bit, describe the gas chambers for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's like, like a room. You think it's a shower with show heads and tiles. You'll be stripped naked. But you know that it's not water coming out, but gas.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in the room with you?

speaker: interviewer
text: How big a room are we talking?

speaker: interviewee
text: One next to the other. You couldn't fall down.

speaker: interviewer
text: They'd push everybody in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody in, and then I don't know whether they had pellets or what kind of gas it was. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Door closes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Door closes. It sounds very bad when the door closes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does it sound like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Have you ever been in a-- heard a prison door close? This is just as bad or even worse because prison door, you always think you can't come out. Here, we knew we wouldn't come out, except that we were so loud despite the fact that the doors were closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And already they had to whip us in there and push us in there, and we made such a racket that everybody heard it. And then finally they said, what is this racket going on there? And this is when they came. But they had put the gas on already but then they said, oh, this is a mistake. It is a mistake. At the moment the gas is turned on, the door is closed, you're in the gas chamber, did you think you were going to die right then and there? No, I wanted to have my birthday on the [? palms. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: What was going through your mind at that moment? I cannot die. I will not have my birthday on the [? palms. ?] This is not right. It is not according to what it was supposed to be. And something will happen. Something will happen. Something must happen. I don't know whether it's an optimism or a rebellion or I don't know. Or not-- I dealt with the reality of it, but I still didn't want it to happen. Something has to be happening between now and when all the gases come. And it did.

speaker: interviewer
text: The doors open up.

speaker: interviewee
text: The doors open up, and everybody fell over everybody else. Not only to get out, but we were so weak already because we had already taken in so much of this gas that we were very, very weak. We were hardly able to get dressed again or find our clothes, never mind we found-- we put on whatever was around. And then we were told to lay down outside. The fresh air would do us good.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take for you to recover from this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was jolted back because I heard my stepfather cough. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The gas as such stayed in us or in me a long, long time-- years.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- we were told anybody who was in the gas chamber couldn't-- had to be very careful when teeth extracted with gas. Don't do that, very susceptible to that. So they said it will stay many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember shortly after the war, a tooth had to be extracted in Hamburg. And the doctor said, very easy to do. We take gas. And then like a routine question, you have never, ba, ba, ba-- you have never been in a gas chamber. Oh yes! This man almost keeled over because he knew I would've-- they would have killed me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think it has anything to do with it anymore today, but at the time. But then also different other gas poisonings after that, so all of this combination of it, I was susceptible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there were any day to day symptoms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-mm. No. It took a little while longer to remember things, like a locomotive which doesn't want to go or can't, doesn't have the speed yet, you know? That's the only thing I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you heard your stepfather cough, did he see you before he went?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no he didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you actually saw him going into the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. Pushed in there, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you to tell your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tried not to and then about maybe six or eight years after the fact, some people were there, and we had company. And somehow it came out, and I felt so bad because I didn't want her to know. And I just said excuse me, please. And I got a little bit of cognac and for her, a glass, and for me, a glass. And I felt so bad that she knew it now. And then she said to me I should have told her earlier. It would have been easier for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you knew that your mother was most likely somewhere at the camp. When did you actually see her?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hamburg, back in Hamburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for the rest of the war you didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too long because in November we were in Oederan in Saxon. And Oederan in Saxon was a camp which was an arm of Flossenburg. And this is where we had to work and where we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were transported there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long would you say your total time at Auschwitz was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: More, more, about that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else about Auschwitz that, that you can recall that stands out in your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to see the corpses being taken out of the crematoriums.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in Auschwitz. And we were asking what, what's going to happen. Are they going to be buried? Why? And where? And that we were told, no, they're being sent to a soap factory. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this is what they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you-- how were you put into this situation where you saw this happening? What was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Probably we were standing some place and we encountered.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the crematorium look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, I don't know. It was not the crematorium. They came out bodies. They were gassed. And one or two people had one over their shoulder, or two of them. It depends whether a heavy person or not. And they were put on wagons. So we said, where are they going? The soap factory. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So after the war somebody once said to me, what happened to your stepfather? And it was not a very kind person. I said, oh did you wash your hands today? And this lady said, what do you mean? I said, you washed it with my stepfather's bones. It's a little harsh, huh? So after two weeks, roughly, at Auschwitz you head off to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oederan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cattle trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had always-- you had been told earlier that bad things were happening at Auschwitz, and you didn't want to go there. How did it feel now that you knew you were leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like one hurdle is behind me. My god, what's coming now?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Be we knew that we had to go and work so that we were not scheduled to be killed. So it still could be very, very unpleasant, you know? We just-- we thought, which was true. But it was behind me. Leave it there for a while. Don't even think about it. You have to do all these little things, manipulations in your mind to, to stay alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you arrive now. How long did it take to get from Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: A day and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the train stops. The doors open. What happens?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got out and, we were-- we were told to go to certain place. And there were not SS men but women, which are much worse than men. And we had quite a few-- I think a dozen of these people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were the women worse than the men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Women are more detailed. If a man says do something, then you do it. And he will tell you do it fast or slow, but he'll not intentionally tell you anything. A woman is more detail-minded, so in other words, to clean-- it's bad enough to clean a place with a small broom or with nothing. But to give you toothbrushes to clean it, that's more or less how a woman would do it and tell you you have to do it with your right hand and not your left hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you get off the train. You're told to go to a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Place, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --specific place. What do you see around you? What are the physical surroundings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Buildings. Buildings. And we were taken to something where they used to have-- to dry material so it was heatable. Sometimes it was so hot, we thought we'd-- phew, you know? And this is where we had these three, these cabinets, work bench. And that was that. There were some other people. There are some people from Poland and some people from Hungary. And they all worked in the factory and so we had to work there too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to communicate with these other people from Poland, from Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't speak Polish and I don't speak Russian. I hardly-- I a little bit spoke Czech, so that was very difficult. [INAUDIBLE] then that they spoke German.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what was your day to day life like at this place?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got something to drink in the morning and maybe a little bit of bread of some kind. And then we were marched to, to the factory, and everybody had a different job to do. And then we marched back again.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, various-- I had to clean the floors. And they taught me how to repair certain machines. And I was really angry because the other people had such easier jobs. They just needed to make other things, but I was angry. But I was glad later because some of their hands were absolutely ruined by the soap or whatever. They had to do-- to do something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what did the factory produce?

speaker: interviewee
text: Munitions rolls, where the munitions comes in. And those were just small pieces of steel. And that this factory had to make it like that you put a hole in there, but it was very difficult. It was very hard, so we had different machines to do that. And these machines would break, and we would have to repair them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know of any, any actions by any of the workers there to either sabotage the munitions or not do them properly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes we did. We decided one time that if we break all the machines or certain machines then the whole procedure cannot go on. So we broke one machine intentionally.

speaker: interviewer
text: We being?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meaning that it blew up. Luckily, we were not hurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was you and who else?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of the girls. We couldn't tell anybody. We had that in my mind. And I don't know whether it helped very much. I'm sure eventually it came to a standstill, but it was not immediately, you know? And then [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I remember-- I don't even know whether I should tell you the story, but it's strange, a small world. We didn't get very much to eat. And there are men [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] watching us with guns, Germans. And this particular man looked like he had something wrong with his stomach or so. He had these lines. He was very skinny.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he called me when I stole some cabbage. And he came immediately, and he said, you're not supposed to steal. You're not supposed to steal that, and why you doing it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I tried to explain to him that when we eat this frozen cabbage, we get sick. And then not only that we don't need to work, but we are not hungry, and that's the reason for stealing that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this man was very kind and he said every day he will bring me a little piece of bread in a certain place that nobody could see it. And I thought it was very nice of him.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the other hand, in the factory when we had to go to the bathroom, we couldn't just go to the bathroom. We had to ask one of these women. Excuse me. Take us to the bathroom. And suddenly there were these little old women around who were also working for this factory. And one of them attached herself to me and told me her name was Anna. And I should be taking the second stall, please, when I have to go to the bathroom. And I should go with my hand to the left side and whatever I find there is for me to eat. And she would bring me every day a little bit of soup and a little bit of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was very good. She had probably one or two more people, which kept us alive. And then was this man who was giving me this bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it came Christmas time. He told me he would go for Christmas, home for Christmas. And he'll be back after Christmas, and he will bring me, if he can, an apple. And I believed him. You know, you want to believe things. And Christmas time was over, and he came back. And his son was with him. He said his son is there. I've never seen the son. And he brought me an apple and a piece of bread. It was a cake, a certain kind of cake you eat at Christmas time. It's called stollen. And so I had to hide it because otherwise you have to, you know, share it with everybody. And when I finally got to eating it, it tasted so good. And I figured it tastes exactly like our housekeeper made it. And then I kind of said this is foolishness. Of course, you want to believe it, you reason with yourself. This man didn't tell me his name. This man didn't tell me where he came from. Every day I found this little bread. Much after the war, many years, two years I think, I worked for the English in Hamburg. And there was a driver, and this driver would take me wherever I wanted to. He was very nice to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I kind of felt very bad because I took advantage of him. And one day I took him out in the hall, and I said, why are you doing this, Mr. Offerman? I mean, I'm really dancing on your nose, for wherever I want to go in the evening, you drive me. You shouldn't do that. Oh, he said, he doesn't do it for me. I said, for whom are you doing it? He said, something from his past. I said, what is it from your past? So he begins to tell me that he was in Oederan in Saxon. And he had to watch a bunch of women and this was a concentration camp. I said, you did? And he said, yes. And there was one and she once stole, wanted to steal cabbage. And he begins to tell me the story.

speaker: interviewee
text: All I could say is, don't worry about her. She's fine. And he said, how dare you, and he really yelled at me until I said, stop it already. It's me. It's me, myself. And he couldn't believe it. He just cried, and cried, and cried. Some years later than that he was a taxi driver again. And I could call him in the middle of the night. He would take me home from wherever I wanted to go. And I was a little far out from Hamburg, called him, he wasn't home. He went over there. He took me home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I had taken-- and I had gotten a whole platter of cake. And as we were in front of the house, my house, I said to him I don't want all this sweet [INAUDIBLE]. Take it home. Where do you live by the way? And he told me the street. I said, ah, that's odd. And he said, well that's a street where only workers live. Who the hell do you know there? I said, which number do you live? And he said, number 22, [GERMAN]. I thought this was odd. It's a small world. And he said what do you mean? I said, well, I know people in number 20. Yes, he has friends there, the Fishers. The Fishers, mm-hmm. So I ask him do you remember the piece of cake you brought me, the stollen? Who baked it? He said, Fishers. Isn't that surprising? We think about it often. Hm?

speaker: interviewee
text: We think about it quite often. I don't know what happened to him anymore. But it was one of these nice people to help us, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: But back to Oederan, we had to do hard labor. We had to take-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] what do you call where lorries go on or trains? Tracks. Train tracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the tracks. We had to carry tracks. We had to do this. We had to do the-- very hard labor and very little food in us. And when, when it was a little warmer, and the factory really didn't work very much anymore, I had somebody that brought me to a man who was a mason. And he taught me how to put up a house, and I had to be his helper. And I did that for a little while. And then one day we were told we  will have to leave, and we had-- we had to go back. We had to be shipped someplace else.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you told this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In April.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you shipped off to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Back to Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll end tape number five here, and we'll pick this up with tape number six.
